title: Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis)
objectives: Recognize the classic presentation of croup
Apply the Westley croup score for severity assessment
Differentiate croup from other causes of stridor
Understand treatment with dexamethasone and nebulized epinephrine

sections.title: Pathophysiology and Epidemiology
sections.content:
Croup is acute inflammation of the larynx, trachea, and bronchi causing subglottic narrowing.

**Etiology:**
• Parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3) – most common (75%)
• RSV, influenza, adenovirus, coronavirus
• Rarely: Mycoplasma pneumoniae

**Epidemiology:**
• Peak age: 6 months to 3 years
• Boys > Girls (1.4:1)
• Peak season: Fall and early winter
• Most common cause of stridor in children

**Anatomy:**
The subglottic region is the narrowest part of the pediatric airway. Even 1mm of edema reduces cross-sectional area by ~35% in an infant.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Classic Triad:**
1. Barking ('seal-like') cough
2. Inspiratory stridor
3. Hoarse voice

**Typical Course:**
• URI prodrome (rhinorrhea, mild fever) for 1-2 days
• Sudden onset of barking cough, often at night
• Symptoms worse at night, improve during day
• Typical duration: 3-7 days
• Peak severity: Night 2-3

**Physical Exam:**
• Inspiratory stridor (biphasic in severe cases)
• Barking, brassy cough
• Variable respiratory distress
• Low-grade fever (usually <39°C)
• Normal voice (distinguishes from epiglottitis)

sections.title: Westley Croup Score
sections.content:
**Scoring System (0-17 points):**

**Stridor:**
• 0 = None
• 1 = When agitated
• 2 = At rest

**Retractions:**
• 0 = None
• 1 = Mild
• 2 = Moderate
• 3 = Severe

**Air Entry:**
• 0 = Normal
• 1 = Decreased
• 2 = Markedly decreased

**Cyanosis:**
• 0 = None
• 4 = With agitation
• 5 = At rest

**Level of Consciousness:**
• 0 = Normal
• 5 = Disoriented

**Interpretation:**
• Mild: ≤2 (barky cough, no stridor at rest)
• Moderate: 3-7 (stridor at rest, retractions)
• Severe: 8-11 (significant distress, poor air entry)
• Impending respiratory failure: ≥12

sections.title: Differential Diagnosis
sections.content:
**CRITICAL: Rule out epiglottitis and bacterial tracheitis**

**Epiglottitis:**
• Toxic appearance, high fever
• Tripod position, drooling, difficulty swallowing
• Muffled (not hoarse) voice
• Minimal cough
• Rapid progression
• RARE since Hib vaccine

**Bacterial Tracheitis:**
• Toxic appearance despite croup treatment
• High fever, purulent secretions
• Poor response to standard croup therapy
• Often follows viral croup

**Foreign Body Aspiration:**
• Sudden onset without prodrome
• Unilateral wheezing or diminished breath sounds
• History of choking episode

**Retropharyngeal Abscess:**
• Fever, neck stiffness, drooling
• Limited neck extension
• Lateral neck X-ray diagnostic

**Allergic Reaction/Angioedema:**
• Sudden onset without URI prodrome
• May have urticaria, lip/tongue swelling

sections.title: Treatment: Corticosteroids
sections.content:
**Dexamethasone – CORNERSTONE OF THERAPY**

**Dose:** 0.6 mg/kg (max 10 mg)
**Route:** PO preferred (equal efficacy to IM/IV)
**Single dose is sufficient** – no need for repeat dosing

**Evidence:**
• Reduces symptoms within 2-4 hours
• Decreases return visits and hospitalizations
• Benefits even mild croup
• Duration of effect: 24-48 hours

**ALL CHILDREN WITH CROUP SHOULD RECEIVE DEXAMETHASONE**
Regardless of severity—even mild cases benefit

**Alternative if Vomiting:**
• IM dexamethasone
• Nebulized budesonide (2 mg)—less preferred

sections.title: Treatment: Nebulized Epinephrine
sections.content:
**Indications:**
• Moderate-to-severe croup (Westley ≥3)
• Stridor at rest
• Significant respiratory distress

**Dosing:**
• Racemic epinephrine: 0.5 mL of 2.25% solution
• L-epinephrine (1:1000): 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL)
• Both equally effective

**Mechanism:**
Alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction reduces mucosal edema

**Onset:** 10-30 minutes
**Duration:** 1-2 hours

**REBOUND PHENOMENON:**
• Symptoms may return as effect wears off
• Observe for 2-4 hours after administration
• May repeat if needed, but multiple doses suggest severe disease

**Disposition After Epinephrine:**
• No stridor at rest after 2-4 hours → Discharge
• Persistent symptoms → Admit

sections.title: Supportive Care and Disposition
sections.content:
**Supportive Measures:**
• Keep child calm (crying worsens obstruction)
• Allow comfortable position (usually in parent's arms)
• Humidified air/mist therapy—NO proven benefit but may comfort
• Avoid unnecessary interventions that cause agitation

**What DOESN'T Work:**
• Mist therapy (no evidence)
• Antibiotics (viral etiology)
• Cough suppressants
• Decongestants

**Discharge Criteria:**
• No stridor at rest
• Normal oxygen saturation
• Adequate oral intake
• Reliable caregiver
• Access to follow-up

**Admit for:**
• Persistent stridor at rest
• Hypoxia
• Toxic appearance
• Inadequate response to epinephrine
• Dehydration
• Social concerns

sections.title: Spasmodic Croup
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Recurrent episodes of croup-like symptoms without viral prodrome

**Characteristics:**
• Sudden onset at night
• No fever or URI symptoms
• Resolves quickly (hours)
• May recur on subsequent nights
• Often personal/family history of atopy

**Pathophysiology:**
Thought to be allergic/inflammatory rather than infectious

**Treatment:**
• Same as viral croup (dexamethasone, epinephrine if needed)
• Consider allergy workup if recurrent

**Key Distinction:**
Viral croup = prodrome + fever
Spasmodic croup = sudden onset, afebrile, recurrent

keyTakeaways: Croup = barking cough + inspiratory stridor + hoarse voice with URI prodrome
ALL children with croup benefit from dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (single dose)
Nebulized epinephrine for moderate-severe croup; observe 2-4 hours for rebound
Westley score guides severity: ≤2 mild, 3-7 moderate, ≥8 severe
Red flags: toxic appearance, drooling, muffled voice (think epiglottitis)

references.citation: Bjornson CL, Johnson DW. Croup in children. CMAJ. 2013;185(15):1317-1323
references.keyContent: Comprehensive Canadian guideline - Westley score, dexamethasone dosing

references.citation: Gates A, Gates M, Vandermeer B, et al. Glucocorticoids for croup in children. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2018;8(8):CD001955
references.keyContent: Meta-analysis supporting dexamethasone for all severity levels

references.citation: Zoorob R, Sidani M, Murray J. Croup: An Overview. Am Fam Physician. 2011;83(9):1067-1073
references.keyContent: Family medicine perspective on outpatient management

references.citation: Westley CR, Cotton EK, Brooks JG. Nebulized racemic epinephrine by IPPB for the treatment of croup. Am J Dis Child. 1978;132(5):484-487

reviewQuestions.question: A 2-year-old presents with barking cough and stridor that is only heard when she is crying. No retractions at rest. What is her Westley score and severity?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Stridor only when agitated = 1 point. No retractions, normal air entry, no cyanosis, normal LOC = 0 points each. Total = 1, which is mild croup.

reviewQuestions.question: A 3-year-old with moderate croup receives nebulized epinephrine at 8 PM with good response. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Observe until 10 PM minimum, then reassess
Discharge at midnight regardless of status
reviewQuestions.explanation: Patients who receive nebulized epinephrine must be observed for 2-4 hours due to potential rebound of symptoms. If no stridor at rest after observation, can discharge.

reviewQuestions.question: Which finding would make you most concerned for epiglottitis rather than croup?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Drooling with muffled (not hoarse) voice suggests supraglottic pathology like epiglottitis. Croup causes hoarse voice and barking cough. Epiglottitis causes minimal cough.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected croup?

options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in croup. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of croup. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing croup, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.

mcqs.stem: A newborn pulse oximetry screen shows 88% on the right hand and 95% on the left foot. What does this suggest?

options.text: Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation
options.explanation: Normally, right hand (preductal) SpO2 should be HIGHER than foot (postductal). Here it's reversed: foot > right hand. This 'reverse differential cyanosis' occurs when oxygenated blood from a PDA preferentially supplies the descending aorta while deoxygenated blood goes to the upper body — classic for TGA with coarctation or interrupted aortic arch.

options.text: Normal variant — up to 10% difference between extremities is acceptable
options.explanation: A >3% difference is abnormal on CCHD screening. Moreover, the right hand being LOWER than the foot is never normal and suggests specific cardiac pathology.

options.text: Standard differential cyanosis from a large PDA with left-to-right shunt
options.explanation: Standard differential cyanosis (lower SpO2 in feet than right hand) occurs with right-to-left PDA shunting. Here the pattern is REVERSED (right hand lower), which has a different differential.

options.text: Equipment malfunction — repeat testing on the same extremity
options.explanation: While equipment error should be considered, a specific pattern (reverse differential) with both readings in clinically relevant ranges warrants urgent cardiac evaluation, not dismissal.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation

coachDx.misconception: While equipment error should be considered, a specific pattern (reverse differential) with both readings in clinically relevant ranges warrants urgent cardiac evaluation, not dismissal.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries wit from Equipment malfunction — repeat testing on the same extremity.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Reverse differential cyanosis — suggests transposition of the great arteries with coarctation.
